metadata.seed: Why do I get so down (depressed?) at the beginning of every semester? It may not be real depression but I do get down and gloomy during the beginning of every semester. I’m in college and have a full time job, so I’m keeping pretty busy. But when I do have a little time to myself, I just want to sit and stare at a wall. It’s not just me; my family has noticed that I act this way too. Why do I do this and is there anything I can do to help? I’m taking 100mg of sertraline already!

patient.query: Why do I get so down (depressed?) at the beginning of every semester? It may not be real depression but I do get down and gloomy during the beginning of every semester. I’m in college and have a full time job, so I’m keeping pretty busy. But when I do have a little time to myself, I just want to sit and stare at a wall. It’s not just me; my family has noticed that I act this way too. Why do I do this and is there anything I can do to help? I’m taking 100mg of sertraline already!

llm_response.response: It’s common for the start of a new semester to feel like a mini‑season of “transition anxiety” – the sudden shift in routine, increased workload, and the pressure to juggle classes, a full‑time job, and personal life can trigger a low mood that feels almost depressive. Sertraline can take several weeks to build up, and its side effects (including a temporary dip in energy or motivation) might overlap with that period of adjustment, so you’re not alone in feeling “down” or wanting to withdraw. Try carving out a predictable, short daily ritual (e.g., a 10‑minute walk, a brief journaling session, or a quick stretch) to give your brain a steady cue that the semester is starting, and consider talking to your prescriber about whether the dose or timing might need tweaking; if the gloom persists, a brief check‑in with a therapist or counselor could help you develop coping tools tailored to the unique stresses of your schedule.
